diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A rate of ~42 bpm is bradycardia by definition; with symptoms it is symptomatic bradycardia.
Nodal-blocking drugs produce exactly this rate suppression.
Hypotension in the context of bradycardia signals inadequate cardiac output — an adverse feature.

symptoms.symptom_name: Near-syncope on transfer with persistent lightheadedness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Presyncope from cerebral hypoperfusion is a defining adverse feature of unstable bradycardia.
Symptoms persist at rest and the bradycardia is sustained, unlike a transient reflex faint.
Confusion, lethargy and bradyarrhythmia are recognised features of digoxin toxicity in older adults.

symptoms.symptom_name: On metoprolol 50 mg BD plus digoxin 125 mcg daily

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Beta-blocker plus digoxin cause additive SA/AV nodal suppression; digoxin can accumulate to toxic levels in frail low-weight older adults.
A digoxin level and renal function are needed to confirm or exclude toxicity.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior myocardial infarction
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Chest heaviness with bradycardia may indicate inferior/RCA ischaemia affecting the conduction nodes; must be excluded with ECG and troponin.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pallor and lethargy, slow to respond

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pallor and lethargy are consistent with reduced cardiac output and hypoperfusion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Several weeks of declining exercise tolerance, acutely worse

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A subacute decline can reflect worsening intrinsic conduction disease before acute decompensation.

symptoms.symptom_name: No true loss of consciousness and no diaphoresis

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior myocardial infarction
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of diaphoresis lowers but does not exclude the probability of ACS, especially in the elderly.

symptoms.symptom_name: 12-lead ECG — slow regular rhythm; characterise sinus bradycardia vs high-grade AV block

investigation_details.expected_result: Marked bradycardia; must distinguish sinus bradycardia from second/third-degree AV block; look for ischaemic changes and digoxin effect
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Rhythm and reversibility remain unknown; high-grade AV block and ACS may be missed.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Only an ECG can distinguish AV block from sinus bradycardia and determine the need for pacing.

symptoms.symptom_name: Serum digoxin level, electrolytes and renal function

investigation_details.expected_result: May show supratherapeutic digoxin and/or electrolyte or renal derangement contributing to toxicity
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Digoxin toxicity as a reversible cause is missed and antidotal therapy not considered.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A digoxin level with renal function confirms or excludes toxicity as the driver.

symptoms.symptom_name: Cardiac auscultation, JVP and capillary refill

investigation_details.expected_result: Regular slow rhythm, no murmur, normal JVP, capillary refill ~3 seconds (borderline)
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Perfusion and volume status not directly assessed; requires in-person clinician.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Borderline capillary refill supports reduced perfusion but is a clinician-obtained sign, sealed from telehealth.

investigation_details.expected_result: To exclude an acute coronary syndrome as the cause of bradycardia
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Inferior ACS causing bradyarrhythmia could be missed.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior myocardial infarction
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Troponin with the ECG is required to include or exclude ACS.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Slow rate plus hypotension plus presyncope plus altered mentation together define an unstable, emergency bradycardia regardless of the exact cause.
Bradycardia plus confusion on metoprolol and digoxin points to a reversible drug cause that must be withheld and investigated.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Characterises the rhythm (sinus bradycardia vs high-grade AV block), screens for ischaemia and digoxin effect, and guides the need for pacing — hospital investigation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Serum digoxin level, urea/electrolytes/creatinine and magnesium
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Identifies digoxin toxicity and electrolyte/renal contributors as reversible causes.
Excludes an acute coronary syndrome as the cause of the bradyarrhythmia.
Screens for hypothyroidism as a slower-tempo contributor once the emergency is stabilised.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Home/telehealth attempt to obtain or interpret an ECG remotely
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Rhythm characterisation cannot and must not be attempted over telehealth; it must not delay emergency transfer.
